coredata.dc:title: Biogenic carbon quantum dots from marine endophytic fungi (Aspergillus flavus) to enhance the curcumin production and growth in Curcuma longa L

coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have investigated the effect of carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs) synthesized from marine fungal extract on Curcuma longa to improve the plant growth and curcumin production. The isolated fungus, Aspergillus flavus has produced a high amount of indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) (0.025 mg g-1), when treated with tryptophan. CQDs were synthesized from the A. flavus extract and it was characterized using ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The synthesized CQDs were excited at 365 nm in an UV–Vis and the HR-TEM analysis showed approximately 7.4 nm in size with a spherical shape. Both fungal crude extract (FCE) at 0–100 mg L−1 and FM-CQDs 0–5 mg L−1 concentrations were tested on C. longa. About 80 mg L−1 concentration FCE treated plants has shown a maximum height of 21 cm and FM-CQDs at 4 mg L−1 exhibited a maximum height of 25 cm compared to control. The FM-CQDs significantly increased the photosynthetic pigments such as total chlorophyll (1.08 mg g−1 FW) and carotenoids (17.32 mg g−1 FW) in C. longa. Further, antioxidant enzyme analysis confirmed that the optimum concentrations of both extracts did not have any toxic effects on the plants. FM-CQDs treated plants increased the curcumin content up to 0.060 mg g−1 by HPLC analysis. Semi quantitative analysis revealed that FCE and FM-CQDs significantly upregulated ClCURS1 gene expression in curcumin production.

head.ce:title: Biogenic carbon quantum dots from marine endophytic fungi (Aspergillus flavus) to enhance the curcumin production and growth in Curcuma longa L

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have investigated the effect of carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs) synthesized from marine fungal extract on Curcuma longa to improve the plant growth and curcumin production. The isolated fungus, Aspergillus flavus has produced a high amount of indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) (0.025 mg g-1), when treated with tryptophan. CQDs were synthesized from the A. flavus extract and it was characterized using ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The synthesized CQDs were excited at 365 nm in an UV–Vis and the HR-TEM analysis showed approximately 7.4 nm in size with a spherical shape. Both fungal crude extract (FCE) at 0–100 mg L−1 and FM-CQDs 0–5 mg L−1 concentrations were tested on C. longa. About 80 mg L−1 concentration FCE treated plants has shown a maximum height of 21 cm and FM-CQDs at 4 mg L−1 exhibited a maximum height of 25 cm compared to control. The FM-CQDs significantly increased the photosynthetic pigments such as total chlorophyll (1.08 mg g−1 FW) and carotenoids (17.32 mg g−1 FW) in C. longa. Further, antioxidant enzyme analysis confirmed that the optimum concentrations of both extracts did not have any toxic effects on the plants. FM-CQDs treated plants increased the curcumin content up to 0.060 mg g−1 by HPLC analysis. Semi quantitative analysis revealed that FCE and FM-CQDs significantly upregulated ClCURS1 gene expression in curcumin production.

Schematic representation of FCE and FM-CQDs and its impact on Curcuma longa L.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcuma longa L. is well known for its curcumin, which is primarily cultivated in Asian countries. Since the emergence of the COVID-19 pandemic, research on the extraction methods and bioactivity of curcumin and other curcuminoid compounds has surged over the past two years (Tomas et al., 2022). The use of turmeric root as a spice in India dates back centuries, as documented by numerous research studies (Rathaur et al., 2012). The current global turmeric market is valued at 4$ million metric tons, with anticipated sales growth of 5.5% through 2033, pushing the overall market valuation past 7.5$ million (https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/turmeric-market). Turmeric compounds, such as curcumin, curcuminoids, demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin have demonstrably significant health benefits (Vo et al., 2021). The turmeric plant and its essential volatile compound having superior anti-microbial and anti-inflammatory properties. But the environmental imbalances influence the production of curcumin, stability, and it has been reported that the high yield of turmeric is a major concern in the species sector (Anandaraj et al., 2014). However, biotic and abiotic variables affect crop yield and reduce curcumin quality (Setyawati et al., 2021). Over the past several decades, 20% of Curcuma longa yield has been affected and global demand (16.1% CAGR) has increased due to environmental stresses (Vardhini et al., 2023). In addition, plant yield has drastically decreased due to invasive diseases, such as leaf blotch disease and Fusarium wilt, and climate change (Singh and Maurya, 2023). These straits can be overcome by nanotechnology and these environmental stresses can be mitigated to enhance sustainable crop productivity (Zulfiqar et al., 2019).

Agriculture productivity by nanotechnology is still promising research and need of the hour. Recent studies have explored the impact of nanomaterials on plants, and some metal and metal oxide nanoparticles have the potential to improve crops (Ahmad et al., 2022). Green synthesized silver nanoparticles using Phyllanthus niruri leaf extract act as reducing agent that effectively control the citrus canker disease (Gaurav et al., 2023). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the most widely studied nanomaterials in plants. Carbon-based nanomaterials are biocompatible, environmentally friendly, and have promising potential in agriculture (Tan et al., 2023). CQDs have the potential to completely transform photosynthesis in plants by serving as effective UV-light collectors. By directing this energy into chloroplasts, they may be able to maximize the absorption of blue light, which is the main source of fuel for CO2 assimilation (Li et al., 2020). Furthermore, CQDs can interact with soil nutrients, making it easier for roots to absorb them. They can also travel through the intercellular space via a symplastic pathway to reach plant cell membranes, where they can initiate specific pathways that increase the amount of secondary metabolites (Khan et al., 2022). Several reports, have proven that CQDs from the C. longa having the high illumination properties and used for targeting the cancer cells and drug delivery (Ilhan, 2023; Vadia et al., 2023).

Fungal species are the attractive agents for biogenic synthesis of CQDs because of their high tolerance to metals and ease of handling. Additionally, the secretion of extracellular proteins by fungi plays a crucial role in enhancing the stability of nanodots (Huang et al., 2020). Compared to bacterial systems, fungal cultures offer advantages such as efficient biomass production and elimination of extra steps for filtrate extraction (Shahid et al., 2020). The mycelial mass of fungi exhibits greater resistance to agitation and pressure than that of plant-based synthesis methods, making them more suitable for large-scale production. Moreover, adjusting various culture conditions, including time, temperature, pH, and biomass quantity, enables the manipulation of fungal metabolism, allowing for the precise control of nanodot characteristics, such as size and morphology (Li et al., 2022; Awasthi et al., 2023). Fungi is well known for its heavy metal tolerance and capacity to internalize and bioaccumulate metals, serve as effective reducing and stabilizing agents (Li et al., 2022). Their easy large-scale cultivation, often referred to as “nanofactories” further facilitates the production of nanodots with controlled sizes and morphologies.

Biogenic synthesis of carbon dots using fungal metabolites is a driving force that increases chemical signaling and nutrient exchange in plants (Ingle et al., 2023). Biogenic synthesis of CQDs is cost effective, non-toxic fluorescent probe which gives controllable size and surface chemistry which determine their properties in cellular imaging and drug delivery (Nair et al., 2020). Endophytic fungi are excellent sources of secondary metabolites associated with plants, algae, and animals. Fungi isolated from marine algae have diverse potential owing to their established partnerships with plants (El-Bondkly et al., 2021). Marine algal-associated fungi can produce allelochemicals that suppress competing organisms, and endophytes from marine algae are untapped resources that produce Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) and gibberellins, which regulate the plant growth and stimulate metabolite production (Elhamouly et al., 2022). Moreover, direct utilization of crude fungal extracts on plants cannot be more effective because of their instability under environmental conditions. Thus, the present investigation was carried out to prepare fungal crude extract (FCE) and fungal-mediated carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs) using fungal crude extract synthesized by hydrothermal methods. FCE and FM-CQDs were applied as foliar spray on turmeric plant and its growth parameters and curcumin production was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of IAA using HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Endophytic fungi was isolated form seaweed Dictyota dicotoma collected from Nochiyurani, Rameshwaram coastal region, Tamil Nadu, India (Latitude 9.267141° N, longitude 79.032900° E). The fresh thallus was collected and removed the impurities by fresh water. Then, it was transferred into the laboratory. The endophytic microorganism was isolated according to the method of Kumar et al. (2022). The isolated culture was identified and submitted in the NCBI (Accession No: OM321442). The isolated fungal culture was grown in potato dextrose broth medium supplemented with 0–50 mg L−1 L-tryptophan at 28 °C for 24 h. The cultures were then centrifuged at 6297×g for 5 min to remove the fungal biomass. Approximately 1 mL of this fungal culture filtrate was allowed to react with 2 mL of Salkowsky reagent (1 mL of 0.5 M FeCl3 in 50 mL of 35% HCIO4) at 28 ± 20 °C for 30 min. After incubation, a pink color developed, indicating the presence of IAA (Patten and Glick, 2002).

IAA from the fungal culture was quantified using High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) Shimadzu, Japan. Initially, the fungal culture was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane filter (cellulose acetate syringe filter) before injection into the (HPLC) system, the quantification was performed according to Muthuraja and Muthukumar (2021). Chromatographic separation was performed using the binary elution method in C18 column. Mobile phase A consist of 2.5:97.5 (v/v) acetic acid: H2O (pH 3.8, adjusted with 1 M KOH), and mobile phase B was 80:20 (v/v) acetonitrile: H2O. The degassed eluents were run for 30 min at a flow rate of 0.8 mL/min. The presence of organic acids in the samples was compared with the peak area of the standard graph.

Carbon dots were synthesized from fungal biomass using hydrothermal method with slight modifications of Flores–Oña and Fullana (2020). One week grown fungal culture was harvested and washed with sterilized water to remove the excess medium and then it was dried in the hot air oven at 60 °C for 3 days. The precursor solution to synthesis the CQDs was made by adding 1 g of the dried fungal biomass in 80 mL of acetone: methanol (v/v; 60:40) to reach a final concentration of 1 g L-1, and then it was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180°C for 7 h. The obtained dark brown solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.4-μm filter membrane to remove impurities and then freeze-dried for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fungal mediated carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the FM-CQDs was recorded in the wavelength range between 200 and 800 nm using a UV-1800 Shimadzu spectrophotometer. The functional groups of the samples were analyzed using an ATR-FT/IR-4700 Jasco make, Japan. The functional groups of the endophytic fungal extract and synthesized FM-CQDs were characterized at wavenumbers between 4000 and 600 cm−1 (Kumar et al., 2022). The fluorescence emission spectra were used to characterize the emission of the FM-CQDs on a Jasco FP 6600 spectrofluorometer at an excitation light range of 260–360 nm. The morphology and distribution of the FM-CQDs were examined by High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) using a JEM-2100 microscope (Japan) at an atomic resolution of 2.0 Å point 1.0 Å line, and the magnification ranged from 58× to 1 million × with an accelerating potential voltage of 300 kV. The crystalline structure of the FM-CQDs was measured using an XRD instrument operated at 45 kV and 40 mA, with Cu Kα radiation at θ–2θ angles within the range of 0–80°C in the Xpert Pro Model, Netherlands (Vignesh et al., 2023).

The pot experiment analysis of plant growth was conducted under greenhouse conditions, temperature was maintained between 25 ± 0.2°C and humidity was 60%. The experiment was carried out between from month of September to November 2023. The rhizome was grown in pots with equal diameters (30 cm) and heights (40 cm) were chosen and each pot was filled with sterilized soil and three rhizomes with an average size of 30 ± 2.0 g were placed (Hossain and Sarkar, 2021). Different concentrations of FCE (0–100 mg L−1) and FM-CQDs (1–5 mg L− 1) were directly applied to the rhizome and three replicates were maintained. FCE and FM-CQDs were applied twice a week and water was used as the control. Plant growth, physiological and biochemical parameters, and semi-quantification of gene expression in C. longa were recorded after three months of treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of leaf relative water content (RWC)
ce:section.ce:para:
FCE, FM-CQDs and water treated leave were collected at the 91st day to analyze the relative water content according to Radhakrishnan and Lee (2013). The fresh leaf weighed and then placed in sealed Petri dishes with distilled water. It was then periodically weighed after gently removing excess surface water until it reached its maximum turgid weight. Subsequently, the leaves were dried in a preheated oven at 70°C for 2 days to obtain their dry weight. Finally, these measurements were used to calculate the relative water content (RWC) of leaves using the following equation.
RWC
( % )
=
(
FW − DW
)
/
(
TW − DW
)
X
100
Where,
(FW) fresh weight of C. longa leaf; (DW) dry weight of C. longa leaf; (TW) turgid weight of the C. longa leaf.

FCE, FM-CQDs, and control-treated leaves of C. longa plants were ground using 80% acetone to isolate the photosynthetic pigments. Total chlorophyll and carotenoid content were estimated using the methods established by Arnon (1949) and Lichtenthaler (1987) respectively. Subsequently, the extracted pigments in the supernatant were analyzed at 470, 645, and 663 nm using an Orion Aquamate 8000 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Shanghai, China). Pigment concentrations were determined using specific formulas.
Chlorophyll a (mg g−1 FW) = [((12.7) × (A663)) - ((2.69) × (A645))/1000 × W] × V
Chlorophyll b (mg g−1 FW) = [((22.9) × (A645)) - ((4.68) × (A663))/1000 × W] × V
Total chlorophyll content (mg g−1 FW) = [((20.2) × (A645)) + ((8.02) × (A663))/1000 × W] × V
Total carotenoids (mg g−1 FW) = ((1000) × (A470)) - ((1.82) × (chlorophyll a)) – ((85.02) × (chlorophyll b))/198
Where,
A-absorption; W- weight of the sample; V- final volume of the extract, and FW- fresh weight.

The lipid peroxidase assay was performed as per the protocol established by Saravanan et al. (2023). The reaction mixture was prepared by combining 200 μl of 8.1% sodium dodecyl sulphate, 1.5 mL of 20% acetic acid (pH 3.5) and 1.5 mL of 0.81% thiobarbituric acid aqueous solution. The 250 mg of treated leaf samples was collected and homogenizing using 10 mM phosphate buffer, pH 7.0, followed by centrifugation at 17,000 rpm for 15 min at 4°C. Then the tubes were placed in a water bath at 90°C and incubated for 60 min. After cooling at the room temperature, 5 mL of a butanol:pyridine solution (15:1) was added for the separation. The upper pink organic layer was separated, and the intensity was measured at 532 nm. Tetramethoxypropane was used as an external standard. The amount of lipid peroxides was determined by measuring mg malondialdehyde (MDA) produced g−1 DW.

Hydrogen peroxide level was determined according to Saravanan et al. (2023). Freeze dried FEC, FM-CQDs and control treated C. longa leaf samples (0.5 g each) were homogenized using 5 mL of 0.1% trichloroacetic acid (TCA) using mortar and pestle and transferred to the centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was transferred to the fresh tube and to the 0.5 mL of supernatant, 0.5 mL of 10 mM potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) and 2 mL of 1 M potassium iodide was added. The samples were vortexed and incubated in the dark for 60 min, the color changes were measured at 390 nm using UV-Spectrophotometer. The amount of H2O2 in the samples was calculated using the standard values of H2O2.

Both treated leaf samples and control leaves were collected and homogenized using a mortar and pestle with 0.01 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The homogenized samples were then centrifuged at 17,000 rpm for 15 min at 4°C. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and used for antioxidant assays. The protein content in the leaves of treated and control plants were quantified as described by Bradford (1976) and a standard curve was generated using standard bovine serum albumin (BSA) with known protein concentration.

Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity was determined using the method described by Marklund and Marklund (1974). The leaf extracts were ground with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.5) containing 10 mM EDTA. Subsequently, 200 μL of the extract was incubated with 200 μL 7.2 mM pyrogallol at 25°C for 10 min. The reaction was stopped by adding 100 μL of 1 N HCl and the absorbance was measured at 420 nm using a UV spectrophotometer. SOD activity was calculated as (%) inhibition of pyrogallol oxidation compared to a control reaction without the enzyme extract.

Peroxidase activity was determined as described by Kar and Mishra (1976) with slight modifications. Control and treated samples of both extracts were homogenized in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 6.8). The mixture was centrifuged at 17,000 rpm for 15 min at 4°C. The clear supernatant was collected as an enzyme source. The assay mixture for POD activity consists of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 6.8), 50 μM pyrogallol, 50 μM hydrogen peroxide, and 0.1 mL of enzyme extract. The reaction was initiated by incubating at 25°C for 5 min. To stop the reaction, 0.5 mL 5% sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was added to stop the reaction. The absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at 420 nm using a spectrophotometer to quantify the presence of purpurogallin.

Catalase activity was determined according to the method described by Aebi (1984). Leaf extracts was mixed with 0.5 mL of 0.2 M hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The reaction mixture was incubated for a specific time (1 min) at room temperature, and the activity was measured at 240 nm using UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Catalase activity was calculated as the amount of H2O2 decomposed per minute per mg of protein in the extract. The unit of activity was defined as μmol of H2O2 decomposed min⁻1 mg protein⁻1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Migration of CQDs in C. longa treated with FM-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of FM-CQDs in C. longa rhizome was observed using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus Bx equipped with C3 camera, Japan). The accumulation of CQDs in C. longa rhizome and the cells was observed in the selected 4 mg L−1 FM-CQDs treated C. longa rhizome. Initially, the harvested rhizome at 91st day was washed with deionized water for three times to remove the soil debris and the transversally sectioned manually and observed under the microscope without adding any specific stains on the specimen, the images were observed and photographed at the 40× magnification (Feng et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of curcumin content in ethanolic extract of dried C. longa by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
ce:section.ce:para: The curcumin content was quantified in the all the treatment of C. longa treated with FCE, FM-CQDs and control using a HPLC system, Shimadzu, Japan equipped with Photo diode array detector with C18 column (4.6 mm 250 mm, and 5 m) used for the separation of compound. The harvest rhizome from all the treatment groups was washed and air dried for 1 week and powdered. About 10 g of the powdered sample was used to prepare the ethanolic extract. For the curcumin quantification, HPLC gradient method was followed as per the protocol described by Khattab et al. (2023) with some modification. Acetonitrile: methanol (70:30) and water with 0.1% TFA was used as mobile phase with 0.8 mL flow and total running time was 30 min, the curcumin peak was scanned at 420 nm. Standard with the different concentration is used for the identification and quantification of curcumin content in the sample.

Semi-quantitative PCR was performed to assess the expression of five genes related to curcumin biosynthesis in turmeric plants, namely ClCDS, CICURS1, CICURS2, CICURS3, and CIActin. Total RNA was isolated using the Nucleo Spin RNA, an RNA purification mini kit. The concentration and purity of RNA were determined by measuring the absorbance at 260 and 280 nm. cDNA was synthesized using the Takara Prime Script 1st strand cDNA synthesis kit. A T100 TM Thermal cycler with the following program was used for PCR: 94°C for 5 min; 35 cycles of 94°C for 45 s, 55 °C for 45 s, and 72°C for 1 min, and a final extension at 72°C for 5 min. The amplified fragments were examined by electrophoresis at 50 V on 1.5% (w/v) agarose gel. The gel was then captured in the UV-illumination chamber (UV transilluminator 365 nm). The amplified fragments from the target genes (ClCDS, CICURS1, CICURS2, and CICURS3) were compared with the endogenous control CIActin.

Each experiment was performed in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. All data were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Dunnet test. The mean values were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05. **, *** represent significance at p < 0.01, and p < 0.001, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative and quantification of IAA production in Aspergillus flavus
ce:section.ce:para: Endophytic fungus (Aspergillus flavus) isolated from the fresh marine seaweed was cultured on the potato dextrose broth. Fungal biomass was harvested and it was dried for further studies (Fig. 1 a and b). Fungal filtrates were used to determine IAA production by qualitative analysis. Fungal culture filtrate was added to the Salkowsky reagent, and the observed color changed from yellow to pink after incubation, indicates the IAA production (Fig. 1d). The control medium did not show any color changes after incubation (Fig. 1c and e). Fungi treated with 50 mg L-tryptophan has shown enhanced production of IAA and it was quantified by HPLC analysis. The HPLC results has shown the absorption peak at a retention time of 5.366 min indicated the production of IAA (Fig. 2 c) in the fungal extract, the fungal culture without adding L-tryptophan was used as a control, and the peak was observed in the retention time of 5.234 with low intensity (Fig. 2b). In addition, standard IAA was employed for HPLC analysis and a peak was found at a retention time of 5.281 (Fig. 2a). IAA production was quantified based on the standard and sample peak retention time, about 0.025 mg/g of IAA was obtained from the A. flavus extract treated with L-tryptophan compared with the control (Table 1 ).

The biogenic synthesis of fungal-mediated carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs) was achieved through a facile solvent hydrothermal method using A. flavus extract (FCE). The synthesized FM-CQDs exhibit a bright blue chromophore under UV light illumination at 325 nm (Fig. 3 b), indicating their potent fluorescence properties. Whereas, no illumination was observed in the control (FCE) (Fig. 3a). Distinct peaks were observed at 225, 268, and 360 nm, indicating the biosynthesis of the CQDs (Fig. 3c). The absorbance peaks of FCE at 268 and 360 nm indicate the formation of FM-CQDs from FCE (Fig. 3b). The synthesized FM-CQDs were prepared using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation range of 260–360 nm. The peak emission wavelength exhibited a direct correlation with an increase in light intensity. The exact light intensity of the FM-CQDs obtained at 445 nm, associated with the excitation, showed a significant increase in light emission (Fig. 3d). The presence of distinct peaks at specific wavelengths indicates the successful synthesis of the FM-CQDs.

C. longa treated with both FCE and FM-CQDs significantly enhanced the relative water content (RWC). The optimized concentration of 80 mg L−1 of FCE increased the RWC (91.7%), and the control plant exhibited 84% RWC (Fig. 9 ). Similarly, 4 and 5 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs also increased the maximum RWC to 92.8 and 94.89%, respectively (Fig. 9). Notably, all FM-CQDs at low concentrations significantly elevated the RWC compared to that of the control, demonstrating their overall effect on the water-holding capacity. A higher concentration of FCE decreased the RWC in leaves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthetic pigment analysis of C. longa
ce:section.ce:para: C. longa treatment with both FCE and FM-CQDs enhanced the photosynthetic pigment with optimal effects observed at specific concentrations. FCE at 80 mg L−1 concentration increased the pigment composition such as chl a (0.60 ± 0.04a mg g−1 FW), chl b (0.50 ± 0.02a mg g−1 FW), total chlorophyll (1.11 ± 0.01a mg g−1 FW), and carotenoids (16.53 ± 2.5a mg g−1 FW) (Table 5 ). Similarly, FM-CQDs at 4 mg L−1 exhibited higher values of Chl a (0.61 ± 0.03a mg g−1 FW), Chl b (0.46 ± 0.02a mg g−1 FW), total chlorophyll (1.08 ± 0.12a mg g−1 FW), and carotenoids (17.32 ± 2.4a mg g−1 FW) (Table 5). The control and high concentrations of FCE (100 mg L−1) and FM-CQDs (5 mg L−1) decreased the total chlorophyll content in C. longa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of toxicity analysis by lipid peroxidase and hydrogen peroxidase assay
ce:section.ce:para: To antioxidant assays (Lipid peroxidase and Hydrogen peroxidase assay) were performed to determine the plant cellular health. C. longa leaves treated with FCE, FM-CQDs, and control plant were subjected to lipid peroxidase and hydrogen peroxidase assay. Malondialdehyde (MDA) content, a marker of lipid peroxidation, was significantly increased in C. longa leaves treated with increasing concentrations of FCE (20, 40, 60, and 80 mg L−1). The MDA content progressively increased from 10.54 ± 1.0 mg g−1 DW at 20 mg L−1 FCE to 14.01 ± 1.2 mg g−1 DW at 80 mg L−1 FCE compared to the control (9.8 ± 0.5 mg g−1 DW), While the MDA was observed higher in 100 mg L−1 FCE (19.86 ± 1.4 mg g−1 DW). On the other hand, FM-CQDs treated C. longa shows lower MDA content 10.17 ± 1.0, 10.79 ± 1.1, 11.06 ± 1.2 and 11.31 ± 1.1 mg of MDA g−1 DW was measured in C. longa leaves treated with lower concentrations (1, 2, 3, and 4 mg L−1) of FM-CQDs, compared to the control treated C. longa (Fig. 10 ). The highest MDA activity (15.73 ± 1.2 mg g−1 DW) was observed at 5 mg L−1, which was significantly higher than the control.

The hydrogen peroxide assay revealed a significant increase in H₂O₂ concentration in leaves of C. longa treated with 100 mg L−1 FCE (7.3 ± 0.5 μM g−1 DW) compared to the control (2.1 ± 0.1 μM g−1 DW). For the lowest concentrations (20, 40, 60 and 80 mg L−1) of FCE shows 3.6 ± 0.2, 4.1 ± 0.2, 4.4 ± 0.4 and 5.1 ± 0.5 μM g-1 DW. In contrast, FM-CQDs treated C. longa showed the highest (5.2 ± 0.5 μM g−1 DW) H2O2 activity. While the lower concentrations of (1, 2, 3 and 4 mg L−1) treated C. longa shows lower H2O2 levels (2.5 ± 0.1, 2.8 ± 0.2, 3.2 ± 0.3 and 3.5 ± 0.4 μM g−1 DW) (Fig. 11 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic observation of FM-CQDs in C. longa rhizome
ce:section.ce:para: The migration of FM-CQD within C. longa cells was observed in the rhizome sectioning using a fluorescent microscope. The selected rhizome treated with 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs, root surface displayed bright green fluorescence due to the presence of CQDs on the surface of the rhizome and white light shows no illumination (Fig. 15 c and d). The penetration of the CQDs in between the cells via the vascular bundle through the xylem cells has been identified in the TS of C. longa rhizome (Fig. 15e and f). The metaxylem and protoxylem shows the bright illumination indicates the presence of synthesized FM- CQDs (Fig. 15g and h). The presence of oil glands in the rhizome was also observed in the TS of C. longa rhizome cell (Fig. 15i and j). There was low illumination obtained in the control treated rhizome it was due to the presence of lignin content in the xylem vessels (Fig. 15a and b). Therefore, the microscopic observation indicates the accumulation and vascular transportation of FM-CQDs in C. longa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of curcumin content using HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The HPLC analysis was performed for the quantification of curcumin content in the FCE, FM-CQDs and control treated C. longa tubers. The peaks of curcumin were identified in the all the treated groups at the retention time of (19.98 ± 1.0 R T) at 420 nm where, conformed with the standard chromatograph (Fig. 16 a). The FCE treatment group shows the, high amount of curcumin was quantified as 0.052 mg g-1 in the 80 mg L−1 of FCE treated C. longa (Fig. 16e). The other concentration shows the lowest amount of curcumin content of (0.025, 0.031, 0.042 and 0.033 mg/g) in 20, 40, 60 and 100 mg L−1 treated (Fig. 16 c, d, f and g) compared with the control treated C. longa 0.032 mg g-1 were observed (Fig. 16b). The FM-CQDs treated C. longa shows the level of curcumin content of 0.060 mg g-1 (Fig. 17 d) and the lowest curcumin observed at 1, 2, 3 and 5 mgL−1 treated FM-CQDs (0.024, 0.028, 0.047 and 0.045 mg g-1) (Fig. 17 a, b, c and e) (Table 6 ). Compared with the both treatment groups, FM-CQDs enhance the curcumin content in C. longa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semi quantitative analysis of curcumin biosynthesis genes using PCR
ce:section.ce:para: The semi quantitative PCR was carried out in C. longa treated and control plants by examining the expression of CIDCS, CICURS1, CICURS2, CICURS3, and CIActin genes (Fig. 18 ). The expressions of all the subjected genes were found to be prominent at optimized concentration of 80 mg L−1 of FCE and 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs. Moreover, the upregulation of the expression of CICURS1 and CICURS3 genes were evidenced by clear bands obtained at 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs treated plants (Fig. 18 L3). The upregulation of CICURS1was noticed in the higher concentration of 100 mg L−1 of FCE, while all other genes exhibited least expression (Fig. 18 L4). Whereas, lower expression of CIDCS and CICURS3 genes was occurred at control and 5 mg L−1 FM-CQDs treated plants (Fig. 18 L1 and L5). The ClActin used as housekeeping gene and it was expressed in all the lanes (Fig. 18. L1-L5). The up-regulation of curcumin biosynthesis related genes clearly shows that the FM-CQDs may impact the curcumin production in C. longa.

The mechanism underlying the utilization of carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance plant growth and curcumin production in C. longa involves intricate processes at the molecular and cellular levels (Nair et al., 2020). The production of Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) by endophytic fungi affects plant growth and cellular metabolism. It plays a vital role in cell division, cell elongation, the regulation of photosynthesis, and the induction of functional metabolism (Kale et al., 2019; Bhatt et al., 2020). In our study L-tryptophan significantly increased IAA production in the fungal endophytes. It acts as an inducer of IAA in many organisms, triggering the key enzymes indole-3-acetaldoxime (IAOx) and indole-3-acetamide (IAM) in IAA biosynthesis (Moya-Cuevas et al., 2021). Similarly, HPLC analysis of IAA quantification by L-tryptophan treated organism Alternaria alternate produce 319.24 ± 14.88 μg g-1 at 500 μg mL-1 concentration (Qiang et al., 2019).

FM-CQDs synthesis using the hydrothermal method showed a lower ecological risk and biocompatibility. The synthesized FM-CQDs emitted bright blue chromophores under UV-light visible spectrophotometry at 325 nm. The fluorescence spectrophotometer emission ranges from to 260–360 nm employed on FM-CQDs and observed photoemission at 445 nm, which indicates the structural composition and doping of the CQDs. A previous study by Chen et al. (2014) observed the emission of CQDs synthesized from Phanerochaete chrysosporium between 296 and 298 nm. Thus, the light emission was consistent with the excitation of CQDs (Tan et al., 2021).

The chemical structure of the synthesized FM-CQDs was determined by FTIR-ATR analysis. The successful synthesis of fungal-based CQDs was confirmed based on O–H and C–H stretching vibrations (Bag et al., 2021). Similar to our findings, Mehta et al. (2015) also observed a broad absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 attributed to O–H vibrations, and 2888, 1765, 1654, 1552, and 1177 cm−1 corresponding to C–H, CO, CC, C–N, and C–O vibrations, respectively. CQDs are the core particles with the less than 10 nm size, due to its nm size with high surface interaction CQDs can easily interact with the plant cells, characterization by HR-TEM analysis provides detailed insights into the size and shape of the synthesized CQDs. In this study, we synthesized ∼7.4 nm size of CQDs with spherical of shape using the endophytic fungal biomass. This finding underscores the importance of HR-TEM analysis, in that CQDs can play a role in various applications, including fluorescence sensing and bioimaging (Ahlawat et al., 2021). Thambiraj and Shankaran (2016) reported that CQDs synthesized from sugarcane biomass were 5 nm in size. The XRD analysis of CDs provides diverse structural characteristics, in our study CQDs shows broad peak at 23.5° indicates the amorphous nature of the CQDs. Similar to our findings, Kang et al. (2018) reported a broad peak at 25°, indicating the amorphous crystalline structure of CQDs obtained by the hydrothermal method. The formation depends on the synthesis method, size, and functional groups.

The efficacy of FCE and FM-CQDs was tested in C. longa. Both treated plants showed maximum plant growth when compared with the water control. The unique properties of CQDs, including their nanoscale size and functional groups, facilitate their movement through the vascular system of plants (Tan et al., 2023). A high RWC indicates healthy plants that are resistant to stress (Giordano et al., 2021). In our study, exogenous application of FCE and FM-CQDs boosted RWC in C. longa. CQDs enhance hydrophilic interactions and attract and hold water molecules. Similarly, the exogenous application of a small nanomaterial and glycine enhances RWC in plant cells and contributes to mitigating drought stress in maize (Shemi et al., 2021). FCE and FM-CQDs play a pivotal role in enhancing photosynthetic efficiency. Our results indicate that FCE and FM-CQDs at optimum concentrations improved photosynthesis and antioxidant enzyme production. The interaction between CQDs and plant physiological pathways contributes to improved photosynthesis, nutrient absorption, and secondary metabolite synthesis (Wu et al., 2022). Through their interaction with chloroplasts, CQDs may act as light-harvesting agents, thereby improving the absorption of sunlight during photosynthesis (Tan et al., 2023). In the C3 cycle, CQDs are degraded by plants and converted into carbohydrates, thereby promoting plant growth (Tan et al., 2021).

The toxicity study evaluated the effects of various drugs on membrane penetration and cell damage. When plant exposed with the stress condition it produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) that can damage cell membranes, Lipid peroxidase, an enzyme in plants crucial for defense mechanisms, when it increases, plant go for the stress conditions (Habib et al., 2020). CQDs may trigger plant defense enzymes, protecting them from oxidative damage by controlling ROS production without causing significant membrane damage, as evidenced by lower MDA levels (Gohari et al., 2021). In our study, FM-CQDs obtain lowest cellular damage of 10.17 ± 1.0, 10.79 ± 1.1, 11.06 ± 1.2 and 11.31 ± 1.1 mg of MDA g−1 D and 2.5 ± 0.1, 2.8 ± 0.2, 3.2 ± 0.3 and 3.5 ± 0.4 μM g−1 DW in 1–4 mg L−1 concentration treated. Banerjee et al. (2021) reported the 25 and 50 nM concentration of CdSe QDs treated Allium root shows the ∼1.4 fold higher H2O2 activity and lipid peroxidase was ∼2.6 fold higher in 50 nm treated than the control. Hence, the lipid peroxidase and hydrogen peroxide assays, can gain valuable insights into the effects of FCE and FM-CQDs on the cellular health of C. longa. In our present study, compared with both extract FM-CQDs shows the lower oxidative damages to the plant cells. The ability of plants to resist stress under (ROS) can be determined by measuring the activities of enzymes such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and catalase (CAT). These enzymes can be activated by environmental factors or by upregulation of their corresponding genes (Chen et al., 2023). SOD is the first line of defense against oxidative damage by antioxidant enzymes and also enzymatic activity leads to generate the H2O2 from O2 in plants (Ighodaro and Akinloye, 2018). POD and CAT are the primary scavenging H2O2 enzymes in plant cells by breakdown H2O2 into water and O2 (Hasanuzzaman et al., 2021). The excess or overexpression of these enzymes is evident in the defense against ROS caused by external stress. Our study revealed that the expression of antioxidant enzymes in C. longa treated with FCE and FM-CQDs at higher concentrations was less toxic to plants, where 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs treated shows higher scavenging activity. This enzymatic stimulation contributes to an overall increase in the metabolic processes. The presence of CQDs can positively affect the ability of plants to absorb and transport essential nutrients (Hu et al., 2022). SO2 strongly upregulates antioxidant enzymes in Arabidopsis (Li and Yi, 2012). In our present study the optimum concentration of 80 mg L−1 of FCE and 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs has shown that it can mitigate oxidative stress by activating the antioxidant defense enzymes, which may protect the cell form its damages.

Plants can absorb nanomaterials through exposure to roots or leaves, potentially increasing interaction between the plant and the material (de la Rosa et al., 2021). Plant cell walls typically range in thickness from 100 to 200 nm, it may the possible reason that CQDs can passage towards the cell wall. Due to their unique luminescent properties, CQDs have various applications, including targeted drug delivery in specific cells. In our present study the FM-CQDs treated C. longa shows bright green fluorescent in the vascular xylem region. Similar to our studies Feng et al. (2019) reported the distribution of yellow fluorescent color in the mung bean root and the tomato root. Li et al. (2022) also reported the penetration of CQDs in the rice plant through fluorescent images. The CQDs enter the root's epidermis and travel through the symplastic or apoplastic route before entering the cortex. They will be moved to the aerial portion of the plants where the water and nutrients flows once they enter the vascular system.

HPLC analysis was performed to quantify the enhanced level of curcumin content in C. longa. Our study revealed that C. longa treated with CQDs at various concentrations showed the highest curcumin content particularly (0.060 μg g-1) was observed at 4 mg L−1 of FM-CQDs compared to 80 mg L−1 of FCE (0.052 μgmg-1) and control groups. This enhancement of plant metabolites might be due to biological changes in plant cells triggered by the interaction of CQDs (Gohari et al., 2021). Similar to our findings, Khattab et al. (2023) reported that various concentrations of ZnO-NPs treated C. longa resulted in the highest curcumin content (715 μg mL-1) at a concentration of 40 mg L−1 ZnO-NPs. This evidence shows that the CQDs and nano materials can easily accumulate in specific cellular compartment in plant cells and influence the polyketide synthases (PKS) and Diketide-CoA synthase (DCS) enzymes for the up regulation of CIURS genes for curcumin production.

Semi-quantitative analysis by PCR techniques is more ideal for the specific targeted gene by appearance/disappearance of the bands in agarose gel electrophoresis (Ben‐Haim et al., 2024). Recent reports on nanodots can upregulates the plant response genes by protecting them from various oxidative damages. Mosa et al. (2018) reported the three targeted genes from Cucumis sativus was identified by high appearance of band in agarose gel image, when the plant exposed to Cu-NPs. In our study the C. longa treated with FCE and FM-CQDs upregulated the curcumin biosynthesis genes (ClCURS1), was observed through semi-quantitative PCR analysis at the specific concentration. FM-CQDs have high potential because of their strong fluorescence and sensitive recognition capabilities (Wu et al., 2019; Salman et al., 2022). Hence, the interaction between CQDs and hormonal pathways may lead to increased cell division, elongation, and differentiation, enhancement of bioactive molecules and contribution to overall plant growth (Wu et al., 2022). Thus, the CQDs may act as elicitors, triggering plant defense mechanisms and secondary metabolite synthesis (Hu et al., 2022).

Our study successfully demonstrated the potential effect of A. flavus crude extract and its biogenic fungal-mediated carbon quantum dots (FM-CQDs) as a promoter for plant growth and metabolite production in C. longa. Initially, IAA production by A. flavus endophytes was screened, and CQDs were synthesized using fungal extracts. These FM-CQDs were characterized and identified ∼7.4 nm in size using UV–Vis, HR-TEM, and XRD. The plant growth-promoting efficacy of both FCE and FM-CQDs was investigated in C. longa, and it was found that CQDs improved the yield. The plant growth-promoting effects of FCE and FM-CQDs were confirmed by a systematic investigation of plant height, number of leaves, and surface area. The FM-CQDs significantly increased relative water content and photosynthetic efficiency. The LPO and H2O2 content indicate the less toxicity in the plant cells. The stress response of antioxidant enzymes at high concentrations can scavenge ROS. Microscopic imaging reveals the biodistribution of FM-CQDs in plant cells. The enhancement of the curcumin production was higher in the FM-CQDs treated C. longa rhizome. Furthermore, CQDs directly penetrate plant cells and induce the expression of curcumin biosynthesis genes. Therefore, the outcome of the present study is an enhancement in the production of Curcuma longa and an increase in curcumin production. Overall, this study suggests that FM-CQDs on C. longa improve plant growth and offers a promising avenue for bioimaging sustainable crop improvement and metabolite production.
